coredata.dc:title: Magnetofluorescent nanohybrid comprising polyglycerol grafted carbon dots and iron oxides: Colloidal synthesis and applications in cellular imaging and magnetically enhanced drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Yu
Xu, Meiyun
Liu, Xin
Jin, Xiaoya
Kang, Jiaqi
Xu, Di
Sang, Houyi
Gao, Peng
Chen, Xiao
Zhao, Li
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional nanohybrids are attracting increasing attention for potential biomedical applications such as bioimaging and drug delivery due to their combined advantages of individual components. However, challenges in the improvement of their synthesis and colloidal stability to facilitate practical biomedical applications still remain. In this work, we report an efficient synthetic approach to fabricate magnetofluorescent nanohybrid (IO-PG-CD) comprising fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IOs) through polyglycerol (PG) mediated covalent linkage in aqueous media. CDs and IOs are first grafted with PG layer, and then functionalized with carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. The resulting CD-PG-COOH and IO-PG-NH2 handled as simple chemical compounds are integrated through EDC/NHS crosslinking to obtain the desired IO-PG-CD nanohybrid. The unprecedented hydrophilicity of PG layer endows IO-PG-CD nanohybrid with excellent colloidal stability in various physiological media, facilitating biomedical applications in vitro and in vivo. IO-PG-CD nanohybrid exhibits low cytotoxicity and its uptake by cells can be obviously enhanced by external magnetic attraction. The internalized IO-PG-CD nanohybrid emits multicolor fluorescence as observed by confocal fluorescence microscopy, demonstrating much better photostability than the nanoparticle labeled with organic dye. Taking advantage of enormous chelating carboxyl groups on the surface of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid, platinum-based anticancer drug was loaded on the surface (IO-PG-CD/Pt) through complexation and delivered into cancer cells in a magnetically enhanced manner, killing the cancer cells efficiently in vitro. Moreover, in vivo cancer therapy indicates that the external magnetic attraction also obviously improves the anticancer efficacy of IO-PG-CD/Pt in HeLa subcutaneous xenografts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetofluorescent nanohybrid
Polyglycerol
Colloidal synthesis
Bioimaging
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid
Cytocompatibility and cellular uptake of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid
Cytotoxicity of IO-PG-CD/Pt and in vitro magnetically enhanced drug delivery
In vivo magnetically enhanced cancer therapy and biocompatibility of IO-PG-CD/Pt

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid through surface engineering of CDs and IOs and subsequent EDC/NHS crosslinking.
FTIR spectra of CDs, surface engineered CDs and nanohybrids.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) 13C NMR spectra of free PG, CD-PG and CD-PG-COOH dispersed in D2O. For 13C NMR measurement, ∼ 20 μL of methanol was added as an internal standard.

(a) TEM images of CD-PG-COOH, IO-PG-NH2 and IO-PG-CD nanohybrid. (b) TGA profiles of CD-PG-COOH, IO-PG-NH2 and IO-PG-CD nanohybrid measured under nitrogen. (c) Photoluminescence spectra of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid dispersed in water under different excitation wavelengths. Inset is a photograph of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid dispersion excited with a 365-nm UV lamp.

(a) Images of EDS element distribution of IO-PG-CD/Pt. (b) Photographs of IO-PG-CD/Pt dispersed in saline, PBS and DMEM. (c) Release profiles of IO-PG-CD/Pt in phosphate buffers of pH 7.4 and pH 5.2 at 37 °C (n = 3, mean ± SD).

(a) Confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells incubated with IO-PG-CD nanohybrid with (M+) and without (M-) magnetic attraction. The imaging region is in the center of the dish. The white dotted circle indicates the attracted IO-PG-CD nanohybrid by magnet. The excitation laser wavelength of and emission collection range for imaging channels are listed as follows: Alexa Fluor 405 (Ex. 405 nm, Em. 425∼475 nm); Alexa Fluor 488 (Ex. 488 nm, Em. 500∼545 nm); Alexa Fluor 546 (Ex. 559 nm, Em. 575∼675 nm). (b) Confocal fluorescence imaging (Alexa 488 mode) of HeLa cells separately incubated with IO-PG-FITC and IO-PG-CD after irradiation with a xenon lamp for different durations.

Cell viability of (a) HUVEC and (b) HeLa cells incubated with IO-PG-CD/Pt and cisplatin for 24 h (n = 3, mean ± SD). (c) Calcein-AM / PI staining of HeLa cells incubated with IO-PG-CD/Pt with (M+) and without (M-) magnetic attraction.

(a) Tumor volume curves (n = 5, mean ± SD, *p < 0.05) and (b) relative body weight curves of the mice injected with PBS and IO-PG-CD/Pt (n = 5, mean ± SD). H&E staining of (c) tumors and (d) major organs from the mice treated with PBS and IO-PG-CD/Pt, respectively.
Hydrodynamic size and surface charge of surface functionalized nanoparticles and nanohybrids.

head.ce:title: Magnetofluorescent nanohybrid comprising polyglycerol grafted carbon dots and iron oxides: Colloidal synthesis and applications in cellular imaging and magnetically enhanced drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection, School of Radiation Medicine and Protection & School for Radiological and Interdisciplinary Sciences (RAD-X), Collaborative Innovation Center of Radiation Medicine of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection
School of Radiation Medicine and Protection & School for Radiological and Interdisciplinary Sciences (RAD-X)
Collaborative Innovation Center of Radiation Medicine of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, School of Basic Medicine, Wuhan University, Wuhan, Hubei 430072, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional nanohybrids are attracting increasing attention for potential biomedical applications such as bioimaging and drug delivery due to their combined advantages of individual components. However, challenges in the improvement of their synthesis and colloidal stability to facilitate practical biomedical applications still remain. In this work, we report an efficient synthetic approach to fabricate magnetofluorescent nanohybrid (IO-PG-CD) comprising fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IOs) through polyglycerol (PG) mediated covalent linkage in aqueous media. CDs and IOs are first grafted with PG layer, and then functionalized with carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. The resulting CD-PG-COOH and IO-PG-NH2 handled as simple chemical compounds are integrated through EDC/NHS crosslinking to obtain the desired IO-PG-CD nanohybrid. The unprecedented hydrophilicity of PG layer endows IO-PG-CD nanohybrid with excellent colloidal stability in various physiological media, facilitating biomedical applications in vitro and in vivo. IO-PG-CD nanohybrid exhibits low cytotoxicity and its uptake by cells can be obviously enhanced by external magnetic attraction. The internalized IO-PG-CD nanohybrid emits multicolor fluorescence as observed by confocal fluorescence microscopy, demonstrating much better photostability than the nanoparticle labeled with organic dye. Taking advantage of enormous chelating carboxyl groups on the surface of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid, platinum-based anticancer drug was loaded on the surface (IO-PG-CD/Pt) through complexation and delivered into cancer cells in a magnetically enhanced manner, killing the cancer cells efficiently in vitro. Moreover, in vivo cancer therapy indicates that the external magnetic attraction also obviously improves the anticancer efficacy of IO-PG-CD/Pt in HeLa subcutaneous xenografts.

ce:section.ce:para: In last two decades, a large number of nanoparticles with various functionalities including porosity, magnetism, and luminescent properties have been developed for applications in biomedical fields such as biosensing, separation of biomolecules, and disease diagnosis and therapy [1]. Nanohybrids comprising two or more different nanocomponents are emerging as new tools in biomedical field. The applications of multifunctional nanohybrids as multi-mode imaging probes and smart drug carriers in cancer treatment have attracted great attention, holding great promise to improve the diagnosis accuracy and anticancer efficacy [2–4].

The toolbox of nanoparticles provides rich building blocks for constructing multifunctional nanohybrids. Among these nanoparticles, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as one of the most promising candidates for biomedical purpose owing to their easy preparation, low toxicity, chemical stability and intriguing optical properties including excellent photostability, two-photon-induced fluorescence [5] and excitation-dependent multicolor photoluminescence [6]. CDs have been intensively explored as probes for in vivo fluorescence imaging, as sensors for detecting biological species, and as nanocarriers for drug delivery [7,8]. On the other hand, superparamagnetic iron oxides (IOs) are biocompatible nanoparticles enabling T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging and have already been used clinically [9]. In addition, the unique magnetic properties of IOs facilitate their applications in magnetic enrichment and separation of biomolecules, magnetically targeted drug delivery, and hyperthermia of cancer [10]. In view of the aforementioned advantages of CDs and IOs, researchers are interested in integrating these two nanoparticles to achieve magnetofluorescent IO-CD nanohybrids for biomedical applications including multimodal bioimaging, photothermal therapy and delivery of anticancer agents. Quite recently, Pramanik and coworkers reported that antibody-conjugated IO-CD nanohybrid was capable of fluorescently identifying multidrug-resistant bacteria and magnetically isolating them from whole blood samples [11]. To date, most of IO-CD nanohybrids have been fabricated through the following two methods: (1) in situ formation by thermo-decomposition of iron-containing organics such as ferrocene and iron-crosslinked chitosan [12], and (2) direct adsorption of CDs onto the surface of IOs [11,13]. However, challenges in the improvement of their synthesis and colloidal stability to facilitate practical application in biological systems still remain.

One strategy frequently used to improve colloidal stability is modification of the nanohybrid with hydrophilic polymers to increase the aqueous dispersibility and stability. In comparison with conventional PEGylation, polyglycerol (PG) grafting has been considered a more efficient surface modification method for inorganic nanoparticles, because PG layer consists of hyperbranched backbone of polyethylene glycol and numerous hydrophilic hydroxyl groups on the periphery [14]. On the other hand, PG exhibits low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility in mice [15,16], and is capable of inhibiting undesired protein adsorption [17,18]. Moreover, through stepwise organic transformations and reactions the hydroxyl groups on the outmost surface of PG-grafted nanoparticles can be converted to more reactive functional groups such as carboxyl and amino groups [19–22], facilitating conjugation with other nanoparticles through complexation or covalent bonding to generate multifunctional nanohybrids. Recently, Zhou et al. demonstrated that the carboxylated PG layer grafted on iron oxide nanoparticles could serve as a template to complex with various metallic nanocrystals [19]. More recently, our group and others constructed magnetic nanohybrids covered with PG layer, which possess enhanced aqueous dispersibility and have been applied in cancer theranostics and pollutant separation [23,24].

Inspired by the previous research, in this work we report a colloidal strategy to construct a new IO-PG-CD nanohybrid for biomedical applications through PG-mediated covalent linkage. The successful synthesis was confirmed by a range of physicochemical characterization. Thanks to the unprecedented hydrophilicity of PG layer, IO-PG-CD nanohybrid can be stably dispersed in water and various physiological buffers. Platinum (Pt)-based anticancer drug was further loaded on the surface of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid (IO-PG-CD/Pt) through complexation with periphery carboxyl groups [21], enabling magnetically enhanced drug delivery. In vitro and in vivo cancer therapy with IO-PG-CD/Pt indicated that external magnetic attraction obviously improved the anticancer efficacy of the nanomedicine.

Succinic anhydride, ferric acetylacetonate, N,N-dimethyl-4-aminopyridine (DMAP) and cisplatin were purchased from Energy Chemicals (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were provided by TCI. Triethylamine, citric acid and urea were obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Glycidol was purchased from aladdin (Shanghai, China) and distilled prior to use.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using reflectance mode. Solution-phase nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) was recorded on a VARIAN INOVA-400 spectrometer. The samples were well dispersed in D2O prior to the measurement. For 13C NMR measurement, ∼ 20 μL of methanol was added as an internal standard. Hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential were determined in water using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a NETZSCH STA 449F3 simultaneous thermogravimetric analyzer under nitrogen in the temperature range from 30 to 600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTWIN transmission electron microscope. All samples for electron microscopy were prepared by evaporating one drop of samples on ultrathin carbon-coated copper grids.

CDs were prepared by a microwave-assisted synthesis according to published literature [25]. Briefly, citric acid (3 g, 15.61 mmol) and urea (3 g, 49.95 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL water. Next, the solution was transferred into a domestic microwave and heated at 700 W for 5 min. After cooling down to room temperature, 20 mL water was added into the crude product. The resulting black dispersion was bath sonicated, and then transferred into a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 8000 ∼ 14,000 Da, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) to dialyze against water for 2 days. The resulting dispersion was lyophilized to give a dark brown powder (140 mg).

CDs powder (20 mg) was homogeneously dispersed in 4 mL glycidol by bath sonication. The dispersion was then stirred at 140 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 20 mL water by bath sonication. CD-PG was collected by centrifugal filtration (Amicon®, 10 KDa) and washed with water 5 times. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation to yield a yellow sticky solid (160 mg).

CD-PG (100 mg), succinic anhydride (250 mg, 2.49 mmol), DMAP (15 mg, 0.09 mmol) and triethylamine (15 μL) were mixed in 4 mL DMF. After being stirred at room temperature for 24 h, the mixture was dialyzed against 50% methanol (v/v) for 2 days to exchange the solvents. Crude CD-PG-COOH was collected by centrifugal filtration (Amicon®, 10 KDa) and purified by washing with 50% ethanol (v/v) 3 times. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation to yield a yellow sticky solid (86 mg).

CD-PG-COOH (10 mg) was activated with EDC (3 mg, 0.015 mmol) and NHS (3 mg, 0.026 mmol) in DMF (5 mL) at room temperature for 1 h. Then, IO-PG-NH2 (10 mg) dispersed in water (5 mL) was added to the above solution, and the mixture was stirred for another 24 h. The resulting IO-PG-CD nanohybrid were separated and purified by repeated magnetic attraction/redispersion cycles to remove unbound CD-PG-COOH. The obtained IO-PG-CD nanohybrid (8.4 mg) were dispersed in water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

IO-PG-CD nanohybrid (20 mg) and cisplatin (5 mg, 0.016 mmol) were mixed in 5 mL water by bath sonication. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 8.0 using 0.1 M NaOH. After stirring at room temperature in the dark for 24 h, the free cisplatin was removed by repeated magnetic attraction/redispersion cycles. The purified IO-PG-CD/Pt (8 mg) was stored in 4 °C for further use.

IO-PG-CD/Pt (10 mg) was dispersed in phosphate buffer solutions (10 mL) of pH 5.2 and 7.4, respectively. The dispersions were shaken at 37 °C in the dark. At each designated time point, 1 mL of the dispersions was taken out and subjected to centrifugal filtration (Millipore, Amicon® 100 K Da). The filtrate was collected for ICP-MS measurement to determine the amount of released Pt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic route of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid is depicted in Scheme 1 . Fluorescent CDs were prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis by using urea and citric acid as carbon sources [25]. Superparamagnetic IOs were synthesized by thermal decomposition of ferric acetylaceonate in triethylene glycol according to reported procedure [27]. Since both nanoparticles bear rich oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl and/or carboxyl groups) on the surface, PG grafting can be readily conducted using these functional groups as initiating points. The resulting CD-PG was further reacted with succinic anhydride to incorporate carboxyl groups on the surface (CD-PG-COOH). On the other hand, IO-PG was surface engineered according to reported methods to convert partial hydroxyl groups to amino groups through stepwise organic transformations (OH → OTs (tosylate) → N3 → NH2) [22,28]. As prepared CD-PG-COOH and IO-PG-NH2 were next conjugated to form IO-PG-CD nanohybrid through amidation reaction in aqueous media using EDC/NHS as a crosslinking agent [24].

To determine the content of nanocomponents in IO-PG-CD nanohybrid, we conducted TGA for CD-PG-COOH, IO-PG-NH2 and IO-PG-CD under nitrogen. As shown in the TGA curves (Fig. 3b), the weight loss in the range from 200 to 450 °C can be ascribed to the thermal decomposition of organic layers on the nanoparticles. The organic contents of CD-PG-COOH, IO-PG-NH2 and IO-PG-CD were estimated to be 85, 56 and 74 wt%, respectively. Based on the data, the weight ratio of IO-PG-NH2:CD-PG-COOH in IO-PG-CD nanohybrid was calculated to be 38:62.

Since surface charge is one of the key factors determining the colloidal stability and biological behaviors of the nanoparticles, we measured the zeta potential of the nanocomponents and nanohybrids dispersed in water and summarized the result in Table 1. CD-PG-COOH and IO-PG-NH2 showed a negative charge of –26.4 ± 4.3 mV and a positive charge of 33.1 ± 5.9 mV, owing to the deprotonation and protonation of surface carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. The zeta potential of IO-PG-CD was measured to be –22.3 ± 2.2 mV, and the negative surface charge would help the nanoparticle to escape the clearance by reticularendothelial system (RES) and benefit accumulation in tumor.[29]

In view of the unique fluorescent property of CDs, we studied the photoluminescence of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid by means of fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3c, the emission peak of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid gradually redshifted along with increasing excitation wavelength, which is one of the characteristic features of CDs. Despite the fact that IOs are fluorescence quenchers, we still observed green fluorescence when the IO-PG-CD dispersion was irradiated with a 365-nm UV lamp (Fig. 3c, inset). The intriguing fluorescence property of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid would facilitate fluorescence bioimaging.

Taking advantage of enormous carboxyl groups on the surface, Pt-based anticancer drug was loaded on IO-PG-CD nanohybrid through complexation with cisplatin under basic condition. [21] The resulting IO-PG-CD/Pt was characterized by FTIR measurement and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in the FTIR spectrum of IO-PG-CD/Pt (Fig. 1), two new peaks at 1540 and 1395 cm−1 were detected, which are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate (COO − Pt), respectively. [21] The EDS element mapping (Fig. 4 a) demonstrated that Pt element distributed homogenously in IO-PG-CD/Pt. The Pt content of IO-PG-CD/Pt was quantified to be 1.52 wt% by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS).

Good colloidal stability of nanoparticles in physiological media is one of prerequisites for their biomedical applications. Fig. 4b shows the photographs of IO-PG-CD/Pt dispersed in saline, PBS and Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM). By virtue of the unprecedented hydrophilicity of PG layer, IO-PG-CD/Pt can be stably dispersed in these physiological solutions without showing any sign of aggregation and precipitation even after standing for a few days. The dispersiblity of IO-PG-CD/Pt in PBS was no less than 5 mg/mL as measured by weighing the nanoparticles after desalting and drying. Compared to IO-PG-CD, the hydrodynamic diameter of IO-PG-CD/Pt increased to 159.2 ± 13.5 nm and the zeta potential decreased slightly to –25.1 ± 0.2 mV (Table 1) due to the complexation with Pt-based drug.

Since loaded chemotherapeutics should be released from the carrier to exert their anticancer effect, the drug release profile of IO-PG-CD/Pt was next studied in the phosphate buffer solutions of pH 5.2 and 7.4 at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 4c, the release rate of Pt under acidic conditions (pH 5.2) is much faster than that under basic condition (pH 7.4). The acid-responsive drug release of IO-PG-CD/Pt is attributed to the protonation of carbonyl groups under acidic condition that accelerates the leave of Pt complex from the carboxylate [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility and cellular uptake of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Since adequate cytocompatibility is required for drug carriers, effect of IO-PG-CD on the viability of human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) was studied by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. S1, IO-PG-CD exhibited almost no cytotoxicity to both cells at concentrations up to 200 μg/mL, suggesting that IO-PG-CD is a cytocompatible nanocarrier for drug delivery. In order to study the effect of external magnetic attraction on the cell uptake, a permanent magnet with a diameter of 6 mm was placed under the glass-bottomed dishes incubating HeLa cells with IO-PG-CD nanohybrid (50 μg/mL). The uptake of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid by HeLa cells was then investigated by Prussian blue staining and confocal fluorescence microscopy. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, the magnet efficiently attracted IO-PG-CD nanohybrid to the center of the dish, showing distinct yellow color. Prussian blue staining revealed that a large amount of iron was present in the cells above the magnet as distinct blue color was observed (Fig. S2). In terms of cellular fluorescence imaging, only dim fluorescence was detected in the cells without magnetic attraction (M-), whereas the cells with magnetic attraction (M+) emitted much stronger fluorescence showing blue, green and red colors, respectively, when excited with lasers of increasing wavelengths. Moreover, the fluorescence was fairly stable and showed no photobleaching even after irradiation with a xenon lamp for 1 h, whereas the cells incubated with organic dye-labeled IOs (IO-PG-FITC) showed almost no fluorescence (Fig. 5b). The multicolor and non-bleaching fluorescence of IO-PG-CD nanohybrid guarantees its application in fluorescence bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of IO-PG-CD/Pt and in vitro magnetically enhanced drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxicity of IO-PG-CD/Pt, HeLa and HUVEC cells were separately cultured with IO-PG-CD/Pt at the Pt concentrations of 0∼20 μg/mL for 24 h. Cisplatin was employed as a positive control. The cell viability was then studied by CCK-8 assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a and b. The viability of both HeLa and HUVEC cells deceased with increasing Pt concentration. HeLa cells were found more vulnerable to the Pt-based drugs than normal HUVEC cells [30], showing almost no viability at a Pt concentration of 20 μg/mL. In addition, IO-PG-CD/Pt exhibited lower cytotoxicity to both cells than cisplatin at the same Pt concentrations, probably because only partial loaded drug was released from IO-PG-CD/Pt during the incubation.

Considering the magnetic responsiveness of IO-PG-CD nanocarrier, we next carried out cytotoxicity assay of IO-PG-CD/Pt under magnetic attraction. HeLa cells were incubated with IO-PG-CD/Pt at a Pt concentration of 0.5 μg/mL, and then a magnet was placed under the dish. After incubation for 10 h and subsequent removal of free IO-PG-CD/Pt in the medium, the cells were analyzed by staining live/dead cells with calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) kit, and then observed by fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 6c, in the control dish without magnetic attraction only a few cells were found dead, and most cells remained intact and were stained with calcein-AM showing green fluorescence. In contrast, nearly half of the cells under magnetic attraction were killed by IO-PG-CD/Pt and stained by PI emitting bright red fluorescence. The results indicate that magnetic attraction can efficiently improve the cell uptake of IO-PG-CD/Pt thereby greatly increasing the killing effect to target cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo magnetically enhanced cancer therapy and biocompatibility of IO-PG-CD/Pt
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect allows accumulation of nanoparticles in tumor through penetrating abnormal tumor vessels [31]. Encouraged by the excellent dispersibility and exciting in vitro results of IO-PG-CD/Pt, we performed preliminary in vivo anticancer efficacy experiments using HeLa subcutaneous xenografts. The nude mice bearing two HeLa tumors on the left and right thighs were intravenously administrated with PBS and IO-PG-CD/Pt, respectively. For the mice treated with IO-PG-CD/Pt, a magnet was attached to the tumor on the right side for 2 h when the mice were euthanized just after drug administration. The tumor volumes were monitored every two days after the treatment, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. In terms of IO-PG-CD/Pt treated mice, the development of tumor on both sides was obviously impeded in comparison with the PBS group, and the tumors on the right side (M+) grew more slowly than the ones on the left side (M-). Consistently, histological examination showed more severe tissue necrosis in the IO-PG-CD/Pt treated tumors with magnet attachment (Fig. 7c). These observations suggest that the anticancer efficacy of IO-PG-CD/Pt was enhanced by magnetic attraction.

Finally, we evaluated the in vivo biocompatibility of IO-PG-CD/Pt. The body weight of the mice was monitored every other day, and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. No significant loss in body weight was observed for the mice treated with IO-PG-CD/Pt. In addition, we observed no significant abnormality for the IO-PG-CD/Pt injected mice in comparison with the control group during the treatment. After the treatment for 14 days, the mice were sacrificed and the organs were harvested for haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. As shown in Fig. 7d, no noticeable signal of organ lesion was observed from the H&E stained organ slices. The results suggested that IO-PG-CD/Pt possesses good biocompatibility, showing great potential in anticancer application.

In conclusion, magnetofluorescent IO-PG-CD nanohybrid was constructed through polyglycerol (PG) mediated covalent linkage (EDC/NHS crosslinking) in aqueous media. The unprecedented hydrophilicity of PG layer endowed IO-PG-CD nanohybrid with excellent colloidal stability in various physiological media. IO-PG-CD nanohybrid emitted non-bleaching multicolor fluorescence, demonstrating great promise in fluorescence bioimaging. Taking advantage of the responsiveness to external magnetic field, IO-PG-CD nanohybrid was internalized into target cells by magnetic attraction and images the cells fluorescently. Moreover, Pt-based anticancer drug was loaded on IO-PG-CD nanohybrid and delivered into cancer cells and solid tumor in a magnetically enhanced manner, leading to largely improved anticancer efficacy. Considering the convenience of PG grafting and further colloidal synthesis, we envision that the synthetic method can boost the development of more multifunctional nanohybrids using other nanoparticles as building blocks for various biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20160329), the Natural Science Foundation of China (31600805, 81671818), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.10.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic conjugated polymer nanoparticles as bimodal imaging agents
Core-satellite polydopamine-gadolinium-metallofullerene nanotheranostics for multimodal imaging guided combination cancer therapy
Core-satellite mesoporous silica-gold nanotheranostics for biological stimuli triggered multimodal cancer therapy
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
Functional surface engineering of c-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Iron oxide MR contrast agents for molecular and cellular imaging
Magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles: synthesis, stabilization, vectorization, physicochemical characterizations, and biological applications
Fluorescent, magnetic multifunctional carbon dots for selective separation, identification, and eradication of drug-resistant superbugs
Near-infrared light triggered photo-therapy, in combination with chemotherapy using magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots for effective cancer treating
Phenylboronic acid-modified magnetic nanoparticles as a platform for carbon dot conjugation and doxorubicin delivery
Enhancing the colloidal stability and surface functionality of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets with hyperbranched polyglycerol for photothermal therapy
In vitro biological evaluation of high molecular weight hyperbranched polyglycerols
In vivo biological evaluation of high molecular weight hyperbranched polyglycerols

Self-assembled monothiol-terminated hyperbranched polyglycerols on a gold surface: A comparative study on the structure, morphology, and protein adsorption characteristics with linear poly(ethylene glycol)s
Self-assembled monolayers of dendritic polyglycerol derivatives on gold that resist the adsorption of proteins
Robust Fe3O4/SiO2-Pt/Au/Pd magnetic nanocatalysts with multifunctional hyperbranched polyglycerol amplifiers
Efficient delivery of chlorin e6 into ovarian cancer cells with octalysine conjugated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles for effective photodynamic therapy
Platinum on Nanodiamond: A Promising Prodrug Conjugated with Stealth Polyglycerol, Targeting Peptide and Acid-Responsive Antitumor Drug
Hyperbranched polyglycerol-grafted superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, functionalization, size separation, magnetic properties, and biological applications
Polyglycerol mediated covalent construction of magnetic mesoporous silica nanohybrid with aqueous dispersibility for drug delivery
Synthesis of polyglycerol mediated covalent construction of a core‐satellite superparamagnetic mesoporous carbon nanocomposite: application on dye adsorption
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Chromatographic separation of highly soluble diamond nanoparticles prepared by polyglycerol grafting
Facile synthesis of superparamagnetic magnetite nanoparticles in liquid polyols
Cyclodextrin supramolecular complex as a water-soluble ratiometric sensor for ferric ion sensing
Effects of particle size and surface charge on cellular uptake and biodistribution of polymeric nanoparticles
FePt@CoS2 yolk-shell nanocrystals as a potent agent to kill HeLa cells
Tumor vascular permeability and the EPR effect in macromolecular therapeutics: a review
